content.text: A pager that cries wolf is worse than no pager at all.

is the ongoing, data-driven process of measuring which alerts are actionable, then deduplicating, grouping, inhibiting, and re-tuning the rest until every notification that reaches a human is worth their attention.
Alert noise reduction is a continuous tuning discipline that treats the alert set as a product with quality metrics. Its core measurements are
(the share of alerts that were genuinely actionable) and

. The process combines four mechanisms: deduplication (collapsing repeated firings of the same condition), grouping (bundling related alerts into one notification), inhibition (suppressing downstream alerts when a known root-cause alert is active), and threshold/condition tuning (adjusting rules that fire without needing action).

For agentic AI systems, noise is amplified by the stochastic nature of models: transient tool timeouts, occasional guardrail refusals, and provider hiccups all generate spikes that self-heal. Without tuning, these produce a constant drizzle of low-value pages that erode on-call trust.

Alert fatigue is a documented cause of missed incidents. When most pages are non-actionable, on-call engineers rationally start to delay or ignore them, so the one page that matters is lost in the noise. Beyond reliability, chronic noisy paging drives burnout and attrition on the very teams you most need to retain. Reducing noise is therefore both a reliability control and a humane operations practice. The goal is a measurable target — for example, keeping alert precision above 80% and pages per on-call shift in the low single digits.
Higher trust in the pager: engineers respond faster because pages are believable.
Lower burnout and attrition on on-call rotations.
Faster incident response as real signals are no longer buried in noise.
A quantified alert-quality baseline that makes regressions visible when a new noisy rule is added.
Inhibition and grouping cut duplicate notifications during large incidents from dozens to one.
Aggressive suppression risks hiding a genuine alert that happened to resemble a known-noisy pattern.
Inhibition rules encode dependency assumptions that go stale as the architecture changes.
Tuning requires disciplined post-incident tagging of each alert as actionable or not — a process cost.
Grouping can delay the visibility of a second, unrelated incident that arrives during the first.
Over-widening thresholds to cut noise can push the detection point past the point of user impact.
Every fired alert is tagged after the fact as actionable or non-actionable, usually during incident close-out. Alert precision over a period is:

A weekly report ranks rules by non-actionable volume. The noisiest rules are triaged with one of four fixes. Deduplication uses a stable alert fingerprint so N identical firings collapse to one open alert. Grouping bundles alerts sharing a label (same service, same cluster) into a single notification with a configurable wait. Inhibition declares that when a source alert (e.g.
) are suppressed. Tuning adjusts the rule's threshold, duration clause, or window so it stops firing on self-healing transients.

attrs.alt: Alert processing pipeline: dedup, group, inhibit, notify, then feedback tagging

content.text: Raw firings enter the alert manager, which deduplicates by fingerprint, groups by label set, and applies inhibition rules before routing a single notification. Post-incident, each notification is tagged actionable or not; the tags feed a weekly precision report that drives the next round of rule tuning. The loop is deliberate: measurement precedes tuning, and tuning is validated by the next week's precision number.
Any mature on-call operation where pages per shift have crept above a comfortable threshold.
After a migration to symptom-based alerting, to tune the new SLO burn-rate thresholds.
When a single incident reliably produces a storm of dozens of correlated alerts.
Not a substitute for fixing genuinely flaky infrastructure — suppression should never mask a real recurring fault.
Instrument the alert pipeline to record every firing with its rule name, timestamp, and eventual actionable/non-actionable tag.
Establish a weekly alert-quality review that ranks rules by non-actionable count and computes precision.
For the top noisy rules, choose a fix: dedup, group, inhibit, tune threshold, or delete.
Configure grouping by service and cluster labels with a short group-wait to bundle storms.
Write inhibition rules mapping known root-cause alerts to their dependents.
Add a duration clause (for-N-minutes) to rules that fire on self-healing spikes.
Track alert precision as a trend and set a target; treat a precision regression as a defect.

# Alertmanager: grouping and inhibition
route:
  group_by: ['service', 'cluster']
  group_wait: 30s
  group_interval: 5m
  repeat_interval: 4h
  receiver: oncall

inhibit_rules:
  # When the model provider is down, suppress dependent symptom alerts
  - source_matchers: [ 'alertname="ModelProviderDown"' ]
    target_matchers: [ 'alertname=~"AgentLatencyHigh|ToolCallErrors"' ]
    equal: ['service']

# Weekly alert-precision report
def precision_report(firings):
    # firings: list of dicts {rule, actionable: bool}
    by_rule = {}
    for f in firings:
        r = by_rule.setdefault(f["rule"], {"actionable": 0, "noise": 0})
        r["actionable" if f["actionable"] else "noise"] += 1

    rows = []
    for rule, c in by_rule.items():
        total = c["actionable"] + c["noise"]
        prec = c["actionable"] / total if total else 0.0
        rows.append((rule, total, round(prec, 2), c["noise"]))

    # Rank worst offenders first: most non-actionable volume
    rows.sort(key=lambda x: x[3], reverse=True)
    good = sum(c["actionable"] for c in by_rule.values())
    seen = sum(c["actionable"] + c["noise"] for c in by_rule.values())
    overall = good / max(1, seen)
    return overall, rows

A platform team ran 62 alert rules generating roughly 90 notifications a week, of which post-hoc tagging showed only 22% were actionable. The weekly report revealed that three rules produced 60% of the noise: a GPU-utilisation threshold that fired on every batch job, a tool-timeout alert that fired on self-healing 2-second blips, and a queue-depth alert already implied by a latency alert. They deleted the GPU rule (moved it to a dashboard), added a five-minute duration clause to the tool-timeout rule, and inhibited the queue-depth alert under the latency alert.

Within a month, weekly notifications fell to about 25 and precision rose to 78%. Mean-time-to-acknowledge for genuine pages dropped from 11 minutes to 4, because on-call no longer triaged the pager with skepticism.
Silencing noisy alerts indefinitely instead of tuning or deleting them — silences expire and the noise returns.
Grouping too aggressively so a second unrelated incident hides inside the first incident's group.
Writing inhibition rules that outlive the dependency they encode, silently suppressing real alerts.
Tuning by intuition without measuring precision, so you cannot tell whether a change helped.
Treating a recurring flaky-infrastructure alert as noise to suppress rather than a bug to fix.
Measure alert precision continuously and make it a visible team metric.
Prefer deleting or fixing a rule over silencing it; every rule must justify its existence.
Use a short for-duration clause to filter self-healing transients before they page.
Review inhibition rules whenever the service dependency graph changes.
Bundle storms with grouping, but keep group-wait short so response is not delayed.

Deduplication and grouping keep notification volume roughly constant even as underlying event volume grows, because they collapse many firings into few notifications. The cost scales with the number of distinct active alert groups, not with raw event throughput. Inhibition evaluation is proportional to the number of source-target rule pairs and active alerts; keep the inhibition rule set small and specific. The weekly precision computation is trivial — a linear pass over the week's firings — and can run as a scheduled job feeding a dashboard panel.

Suppression mechanisms are safety-critical: a mis-scoped inhibition or an over-broad silence can hide a real outage. Require code review for inhibition and silence changes, and expire silences by default. Emit a metric for the number of alerts currently suppressed and alert if it grows unexpectedly — a suppression spike can itself indicate a problem or a misconfiguration. Keep the tagging data (which alerts were actionable) access-controlled but auditable, since it drives decisions about what gets suppressed.

Treat the alert set like a spam filter you tune continuously. A filter that blocks everything is useless, and one that lets everything through is noise; the value is in the feedback loop where each message is marked spam or not, and the filter adapts. Precision is your inbox's signal quality. Deduplication and grouping are the filter collapsing a hundred identical newsletters into one thread; inhibition is muting a thread you already know about. The discipline is not a one-time cleanup but a weekly habit.

Tuning is most effective when it follows the weekly precision report rather than intuition. Consider a rule that pages on a raw 5% tool-error rate with no duration clause. The report shows it fired 22 times last week, 3 actionable. The fix is not to delete it — tool errors matter — but to add a duration clause so it only pages on sustained elevation, and to convert the transient version into a ticket. This preserves the signal for real incidents while removing the self-healing blips that generated the noise.

After each change, the following week's precision number is the verdict. If precision for the rule rises and no real incident was missed, the change stands; if a genuine incident slipped through, the duration clause was too long and is shortened. The loop is empirical, not a one-time guess.

This kind of replay against historical firing data lets a team choose a duration clause with evidence rather than guesswork, confirming that the chosen window removes the transient noise while still catching every real incident in the replay set.
Drive tuning from the precision report, not from the most recent annoying page.
Prefer adding a duration clause or downgrading to ticket over deleting a rule that guards a real risk.
Replay a candidate change against historical firing data to confirm it misses no real incidents.
Let the next week's precision number be the verdict on each change.
Keep a changelog of tuning decisions so a reverted change is not silently reintroduced.
Escalate rules that stay noisy after tuning to the owning team as a reliability bug.

attrs.alt: A storm of correlated firings collapsing to a single actionable notification

content.text: During a large incident the raw firing count can spike into the dozens as every dependent symptom trips at once. Grouping collapses those sharing a service label into one notification, and inhibition mutes the dependents of a known root cause, so on-call receives a single, coherent page instead of a pager storm. The goal is one incident, one thread — not one incident, forty pages.
Measure alert precision and pages-per-shift; you cannot tune what you do not measure.
Four levers reduce noise: deduplication, grouping, inhibition, and threshold/duration tuning.
Prefer fixing or deleting a rule over silencing it; silences hide problems and expire.
Add for-duration clauses so self-healing transients never reach the pager.
Review inhibition rules as the architecture changes to avoid suppressing real alerts.
Noise reduction is a continuous weekly loop, not a one-off cleanup project.
